title: Robert Dabney
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson

outline.title: Introduction: An Unenviable Task
subpoints: Elder Nick Anderson acknowledges the difficulty: Dabney was a slaveholder, a defender of slavery, a Confederate officer, and deeply racist — yet also a brilliant theologian, a prophetic voice on education, and highly regarded by his peers
A.A. Hodge called Dabney "the greatest teacher of theology in the United States, if not in the world"

Framing principle: moral perfection is not required to learn from someone; if it were, we could learn only from Christ; we learn from David, Abraham, Peter, Noah, and Paul — all of whom were deeply flawed

outline.title: The Life of Robert Lewis Dabney
subpoints: Born March 5, 1820, in Virginia; died 1898; father died when Dabney was 13 — this gave him a profound, lifelong sense of duty and purpose
Called by one biographer "a type of English Puritan in his day" — he felt God had something definite for him to do and never lost that conviction
Educated at Hampton-Sydney College and University of Virginia (master's degree, 1842 at age 22); first pastorate at Tinkling Spring (Scotch-Irish congregation) 1847–1853 — not without difficulty

Professor at Union Theological Seminary in Virginia for 30 years (1846–1876); taught over 500 students; built the school almost from nothing; declined Charles Hodge's personal plea to come to Princeton because he felt called to the South
Served under Stonewall Jackson in the Civil War (1862); Jackson called him "the most efficient officer I know"; credited by some with saving a battle through his knowledge of topography
Later moved to Texas in 1883; founded the moral philosophy department at the University of Texas and the Austin School of Theology
Suffered a urinary infection and surgery that went wrong; lost his sight; endured total blindness and constant pain for the last 11 years of his life — but continued to lecture and write

A.A. Hodge's statement: when introducing systematic theology to students, he would list many books and then come to the last one and say, "I want to call your attention to one other work — it is by the profoundest thinker and writer on theological subjects in my judgment that America has produced." That work was Dabney's systematic theology

outline.title: Dabney on Secularized Education
subpoints: After the Civil War, a movement arose to have government-run, "neutral perspective" public schools; William Ruffner — a Presbyterian minister, Dabney's former classmate, appointed superintendent of public instruction in Virginia — argued the state had a moral obligation to educate its citizens
Dabney opposed this on the grounds that the parent's providential authority over the child is paramount: "Character itself at the outset of manhood is mainly determined by the parents"

Six arguments against secular education: (1) no civilization in history has ever had neutral education — it has always been tied to religion; (2) true education is a spiritual process — how can a neutral education nurture a soul?; (3) if secular education is consistently non-Christian, its most important branches must be mutilated or omitted; (4) the will is executive and the conscience is directive — how can secular education form conscience?; (5) the best teachers are true Christians who carry religion into everything; (6) a non-Christian training is literally an anti-Christian training

Dabney's prophecy: "All prayers, catechisms, and Bibles will ultimately be driven out of the schools. Humanity always finds out sooner or later that it cannot get on without a religion, and it will take on a false one in preference to none. Infidelity and practical ungodliness will become increasingly prevalent among Protestant youth and our churches will have a more arduous contest for growth if not for existence."
Elder Anderson notes that Dabney was right: public schools have become completely secularized, have taken on a different religion, and the prediction has been fulfilled

outline.title: Dabney's Flaws: Slavery and Racism
subpoints: Dabney was a slaveholder who tried to defend slavery from the Bible; Elder Anderson attempts to read some of Dabney's defenses of slavery and finds it "literally and figuratively hard to read" — baffling that it came from the same man who wrote so profoundly elsewhere
Immediately after the Civil War, Dabney published The Defense of Virginia and Through Her the South (1867) — an unvarnished account of his views

Why not just erase him from history? If learning about Dabney perpetuated racism, then ignoring the mistakes of the past only makes us more likely to repeat them; we must reckon honestly with both the good and the bad

We do not know anyone else who can teach us that was not flawed like Dabney — King David, Abraham, Job, Peter, Noah, Paul; it is easy to judge Dabney 150 years after the fact but impossible to put ourselves in his shoes

Self-examination: Dabney's flaws remind us that we are sinful people entirely dependent on the grace of God; we should ask, when people look back at us in 100 years, what sins will they wonder how we were blind to?

summary:
Elder Nick Anderson presents the life and legacy of Robert Lewis Dabney (1820–1898), the concluding figure in the Reformed Church Biographies series. The lesson opens with a frank acknowledgment of the difficulty: Dabney was brilliant, deeply principled, and prophetically insightful on certain issues, yet also a slaveholder, a defender of slavery, and a racist. A.A. Hodge — Charles Hodge's son — considered Dabney the greatest theological thinker America had ever produced, more profound than even Jonathan Edwards. Yet Dabney served as a Confederate officer under Stonewall Jackson and wrote a post-war defense of slavery and the Confederate cause.

The bulk of the lesson focuses on Dabney's remarkably prescient critique of government-run secular education, drawn from his essay on "secularized education." Dabney argued in six detailed points that truly neutral education is impossible, that education is inherently religious, and that handing children over to the state for education removes God-given parental authority. He predicted, against the popular opinion of his day, that public schools would eventually expel prayer, catechisms, and the Bible; would take on a false religion in place of Christianity; and would produce increasing infidelity among Protestant youth. Elder Anderson notes with sobering clarity that this prediction has proven accurate.

The lesson closes with a call for honest reckoning with Dabney's profound failures: we cannot whitewash history, nor should we erase it. Instead, we learn from it — we oppose what was wrong, we benefit from what was true, and we soberly ask ourselves what sins of our own day we may be as blind to as Dabney was to the evil of slavery.
transcript:
## Opening Prayer

Well this morning I have the unenviable task of teaching you about Robert Dabney, one of the famous Southern Presbyterians. Last week we learned about the other Southern Presbyterian in our series, James Henley Thornwell. And if you were uncomfortable with Thornwell, you have my apologies. But before I go any further, let's pray.

Our gracious God, we thank you that you are a gracious God and that you work in the lives of men who are like us — who are flawed in ways that are sometimes hard for us to understand. But Lord, we thank you that you work through us anyway, and we pray that you would enable me to speak well and that we would learn something from the life of Robert Dabney this morning. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.

## The Life of Robert Lewis Dabney

So before I say anything else to prejudice you against Dabney, let's just take a look at his life. He was born and raised in Virginia. He was born on March 5th, 1820. He was the third of six children. He died in 1898, so he almost made it into the 20th century. He had some type of acute illness at the end of his life — I'll get into that later.

His family growing up was neither rich nor poor — middle class, you could say. His father, however, died at the age of 48, when Dabney was only 13 years old. This would have a profound and lasting effect on his life. At that age, he had a strong sense of duty that he carried with him for his entire life. He became, in the words of one biographer, "a type of English Puritan in his day." He had a very strong sense that God had something for him to do — a definite purpose — and that stayed with him.

He was educated first by his mother, who taught him how to spell, and then his first formal teacher was his older brother Charles, who taught him in primary school. There he studied Latin and Greek, and it was quite rigorous. To give you an idea of how rigorous it was — one of the books they used to study Greek was written in Latin. I can't even wrap my head around that.

He entered Hampton-Sydney College at age 16 as a sophomore. He left there in part because he was not impressed with the language professors. He earned his master's degree from the University of Virginia in 1842 at the age of 22.

As a pastor he served the Scotch-Irish congregation at Tinkling Spring for six years, from 1847 to 1853. This was not without difficulties — if you know anything about Scotch-Irish. His biographer says: before he had been there two years, there was a difference of opinion about building a new building. And Dabney said: "The Scotch-Irish are the most inflexible people in the world when they're right, and the most vexatiously pigheaded and foolish when wrong." That gives you a little bit of insight into what Dabney was like as a person — not one to mince words, with a fiery personality at times. But generally he was known to his students and congregants as being a very gracious person — very kind, very loving toward the people who knew him.

As a pastor at this first church, he experienced depression due to his perception that his ministry was unfruitful. At one point they went an entire year without seeing a single convert or a single person joining the church by transfer of membership. Eventually, however, the church experienced something of a revival and added around 30 or 35 people in a pretty short period of time. And during this first period of pastoral ministry, he became known as an honest man, an able preacher, and a good pastor. Some said that aside from Thornwell — or perhaps after Thornwell — Dabney was the most powerful preacher in the United States at that time.

He was also a father. He married and had six children; three of his children died at a young age, which was not uncommon then. But the death of his own children had a profound effect on him — similar to the earlier death of his father. His students noticed that his prayer life changed, that he was a little more tender in his prayers.

As an author, he wrote extensively on a very wide range of subjects. How many of you have actually read something by Dabney? So about three or four people. How many of you have actually heard the name Dabney before? Okay, roughly half. He wrote a systematic theology — probably his most famous work, which you would read at seminary. He wrote on education, on Darwinism, on geology and creation. On Darwinism he was a stalwart: "Darwinism happens just now to be the current manifestation which the fashion of the day gives to the permanent anti-theistic tendency in sinful men. As long as men do not like to retain God in their knowledge, the objection to the argument for existence will reappear in some form."

On the day-age theory and the gap theory — both invoked in his day — he rejected them outright. Interestingly, one notable adherent to the day-age theory was Charles Hodge. Dabney knew the Bible had been challenged intellectually many times in the past and that it would stand against these theories. He said: "If any part of the Bible must wait to have its real meaning imposed upon it by another and a human science, that part is at least meaningless and worthless to our souls. It must expound itself independently, making other sciences its servants, not its masters."

He also wrote on slavery, history, politics, the relationship of seminaries to churches, the morality of the legal profession, Stonewall Jackson's biography, philosophy, dancing, instrumental music in public worship, reasoning and understanding, inductive logic, what he called "spurious religious excitements," and even communism.

He was completely unwavering in his commitment to what he felt God had called him to do. He had a reputation for never dodging a hard problem.

He was also an architect — he designed the plans for his first church, and there are two other buildings he designed that are still standing today.

He served as a professor at Union Theological Seminary in Virginia for 30 years, from 1846 to 1876, building it up almost from nothing. He taught over 500 students. Later in life he moved to Texas in 1883, became a professor at the University of Texas, founded the moral philosophy department there, and started the Austin School of Theology.

He also served in the Civil War under Stonewall Jackson in 1862. Jackson called him "the most efficient officer that he knew." There is an account of Dabney — who was in the hospital, officially on leave because he was sick — overhearing an orderly who did not understand a message he was supposed to deliver. From memory, and using his extensive knowledge of the topography of the land, Dabney figured out what was really going on and some people credit him with saving one of the battles for the Confederacy because of that.

At the end of his life, he suffered from a urinary infection and a bronchial infection. The urinary infection was operated on, but the surgery only made things worse. And for the last 15 years of his life he was in constant pain. After the surgery, he also lost his sight, and he suffered from total blindness for the last 11 years of his life. But as you can imagine, a person like Dabney did not stop working — he continued to lecture and write as much as he could.

When Dabney was 40 years old, Charles Hodge sent him a letter begging and pleading for Dabney to come teach at Princeton Seminary. Dabney did not accept. He felt that if he went to Princeton he would immediately become far more famous, but he felt called to the South. He actually said: "The North has plenty of able ministers, and the South doesn't." So he decided to stay where he saw the need.

A.A. Hodge — Charles Hodge's son — called Dabney "the greatest teacher of theology in the United States, if not in the world." In fact, when A.A. Hodge was introducing systematic theology to his students, he would go down a list of books and then come to the last one and say something like this: "I want to call your attention to one other work." And then he'd say: "It is by the profoundest thinker and writer on theological subjects, in my judgment, that America has produced. I beg you to read it." And the work was Dabney's systematic theology.

Most of us, when we think of the most profound theological thinker in the United States, might say Jonathan Edwards. But A.A. Hodge thought Dabney was more profound than Edwards.

His students said he was the godliest person they ever knew. One student said he was like the Apostle John and that he strove to be like his Master. His students said he was dogmatic, he was clear, and he was very zealous for the truth. He gave not only his truth but himself as well. Ian Murray, in his biography of Dabney, says that in his estimation Dabney may have been the most courageous person he was aware of outside the pages of Scripture.

## Dabney on Secularized Education

So clearly Dabney was highly thought of by the people who knew him. That is one reason he is worth learning about. But as you know, he was a flawed character.

A wise man once wrote: to sort out the past, we need to understand multiple sources of historical influence. To sort out the present, we need an accurate knowledge of the past. As uncomfortable as it may be to learn about men like Thornwell and Dabney — who were slaveholders and racists — we do not do ourselves any favors by ignoring them, by whitewashing them, or by pretending they did not exist. We certainly do not have to agree with them on all points in order to learn something from them.

In fact, if we ignore the mistakes of the past, there is a good chance we will repeat those errors. And if we ignore them or whitewash them, we create a version of history that is a lie. As I researched Dabney, I came across comments online that said something to the effect of: "No Christian should read Dabney; he should just be erased from history, because if we read Dabney, that will perpetuate slavery and racism." I doubt anybody today is going to go home and Google "where do I buy slaves." If anything, learning about Dabney and men who were deeply flawed in certain ways will actually help us not to make the same mistakes.

So why talk about Dabney at all? Well, one reason is that he probably should be more widely known than he is — but when you are on the losing side of the Civil War and on the losing side of the slavery debate, your name is probably not going to be well known.

If moral perfection is required for us to learn from someone, we cannot learn from anyone but Jesus Himself. Secondly, as you have seen, Dabney was a uniquely gifted theologian. He made every attempt to apply theology to real life.

To demonstrate this, I want to look at something he wrote called "Secularized Education." If you want to read a good article, there is a fairly short e-book called *Dabney Takes America to School*, which I am drawing from in this portion of the lesson.

After the Civil War, there was a movement to have government-run schools with a "neutral perspective." This did not exist in Virginia before the war. Dabney was strongly opposed to it. What is interesting is that William Ruffner — also a Presbyterian minister, Dabney's former classmate, appointed superintendent of public instruction in the state of Virginia — argued the opposite position: that the state had a moral obligation to educate its citizens. So these two men, both believers, both Presbyterian ministers, debated this publicly.

For Dabney, the influence of the parent on the child was paramount. He said: "Character itself at the outset of manhood is mainly determined by the parents, and that chiefly by their example." And: "There is no power allowed to any creature under heaven over another responsible creature so wide as this providential power of the parent."

Here is what Dabney says on the importance of parenting — and I want to quote him at length because I cannot summarize it:

> The education of children for God is the most important business on earth. It is the only business for which the earth exists. To it all politics, all war, all literature, all money-making ought to be subordinated. And every parent especially ought to feel every hour of the day that next to his own making — next to making his own calling and election sure — this is the end for which he has been kept alive by God. This is his task on earth. On the right training of the generation now arising turns not only the individual salvation of each member of it, not only the religious hope of the age which is approaching, but the fate of all future generations in a large degree. Train up him who is now a boy for Christ and you not only sanctify that soul, but you set on foot the best earthly agencies to redeem the whole broadening stream of human beings who shall proceed from him down to the time when men cease to marry and give in marriage. Until then, the work of education is never-ending.

You can see why I did not want to try to summarize that. A quite profound statement about the importance of parents in educating their children or at least being in charge of their education.

Dabney clearly did not believe it was the right of the state to control education. He argued that if the state is the educator, education must be completely secularized because the US had severed itself from particular churches and religions. And Dabney would go on to argue that secularized education is not even possible at all because, as he put it, "education is the nurture and development of the whole man for his proper end." And what is the chief end of man? To glorify God and to enjoy Him forever. Clearly, secular education cannot do that.

In his book, Dabney gives six reasons why secular education is not even possible:

1. No people in any age, religion, or civilization before ours has ever thought so. Education has always been tied to religion.
2. True education is in a sense a spiritual process — a nurture of the soul. How can a neutral education nurture a soul?
3. If secular education is made consistently and honestly non-Christian, then all its most important branches must be omitted or must undergo a mutilation and falsification far worse than absolute omission.
4. Of all rightful human action, the will is the executive and the conscience the directive faculty — and secular education cannot form conscience.
5. We need the best men to teach our children. The best are true Christians who carry their religion into everything. Why would we willingly submit our own children to unbelievers for their education?
6. To every Christian citizen, the most conclusive argument against secularized education is contained in his own creed touching human responsibility. A non-Christian training is literally an anti-Christian training.

And then Dabney goes on to predict what will come of these state-run schools. He says: "But nearly all public men and devisers declare that the state schools are the glory of America, that they are our finality and in no event to be surrendered. And we have seen that their complete secularization is logically inevitable." And then:

> Christians must prepare themselves then for the following results: all prayers, catechisms, and Bibles will ultimately be driven out of the schools. Humanity always finds out sooner or later that it cannot get on without a religion, and it will take on a false one in preference to none. Infidelity and practical ungodliness will become increasingly prevalent among Protestant youth and our churches will have a more arduous contest for growth if not for existence.

I think he was right. And this is one of the reasons why if we just decide we are going to throw this guy out and not consider anything he said because we do not like his views on slavery, I think we have lost something. Dabney was one of the few people who saw that coming.

Dabney returns to the duty of parents: "Though parents are fallen and imperfect, the supreme authority must be placed somewhere. Someone has to have the authority. And God has indicated that no place is so safe for it as in the hands of parents who have the supreme love for the child and superior opportunity." And: "God has immediately and authoritatively instituted three organisms for man on earth — the state, the visible church, and the family. And these are coordinate in rights and mutual independence. The church or state has no more right to invade the parental sphere than the parent has to invade theirs."

## Conclusion: What Do We Do with Dabney?

Dabney provides valuable insight on secularized education as well as on other topics. He was able to see through the smoke screen and get to the root of the issues of his day because he knew his Bible. And he was able to apply that to everything from education to evolution to geology. Dabney stood firm on orthodoxy. To the extent that some credit him with single-handedly saving the tide of liberalism from invading the southern church as it did the northern church.

So if we ignore him, we are just whitewashing history and we are not necessarily better off for it. Dabney was a brilliant man. He was a gifted theologian. And if he had not been on the wrong side of slavery, if he had not been on the wrong side of the Civil War, he would probably be far more famous and influential than he is today.

So what are we supposed to do here? On the one hand, he is a great theologian from whom we can learn a lot. On the other hand, he was a slaveholder who tried to defend slavery from the Bible.

Let's think for a moment. Do we know anyone else we can learn from who was flawed like Dabney? King David — a man after God's own heart, who committed adultery and was a murderer. Abraham — a coward and a liar when it came to his wife Sarah. Job — who had the audacity to question God. Peter — who denied Christ right before the crucifixion. Noah — who got drunk. Paul — the greatest evangelist the world has ever known, who persecuted Christianity before he was converted.

It is easy to judge Dabney 100, 150 years after the fact. But it is impossible for us to put ourselves in his shoes. It is very difficult for us to understand the world in which he lived. We do not really have an experience comparable to the one he grew up in.

So instead of judging him by today's standards, as Pastor Tito said last week, we can learn from him and make sure we do not make the same mistakes. Dabney's flaws remind us that we are sinful people who are 100% relying on the grace of God because we sin in ways we do not even recognize. We too need to be reminded that we are blind to our own sin. We need to examine ourselves regularly, see if we are walking in any sinful ways, and ask the Lord to show us these things so that we might know them.

When we look back at Dabney, when we look back at Thornwell, it is easy to shake our heads and wonder how they could be so blind to the evils of slavery. But a perhaps more sobering consideration would be this: when people look back at us in a hundred years, what sins are they going to wonder about — that we committed, that we were blind to?

If you are in the book study groups, one of the chapters was on self-examination. And I think that is something we can take away from this today. We need to examine ourselves and be honest about what ways we are sinning and failing, instead of judging people from the past. We can use that to humble ourselves.

Any questions?

*[Discussion follows: on reading living authors who are flawed; on Dabney's prophetic character regarding public schools; on whether Hodge lost admiration for Dabney after the Civil War; on learning from CS Lewis while holding the Bible open; on opposing false ideas while still being discerning about who we read.]*

Let's pray. Gracious God, we thank you that you are a good and merciful God to us — us who are full of flaws, who are full of sinful tendencies, who are at times very much blind to our own sins. We thank you that you are gracious to us in the Lord Jesus Christ, that we are saved by Him and by His grace alone, not by our own works. We thank you for Dabney. We thank you for the good that he wrote. We pray that you would help us to benefit from him and from other men like him who have written good things for our benefit. We ask this all in Christ's name. Amen.
